meta.title: CRR Learn
meta.subtitle: Build regulatory judgement under CRR / CRR3
meta.intro: A short course in reading credit-risk regulation the way a prudent bank researcher does — checking source status, date, jurisdiction and transitionals before trusting a proposition. Each question gives you the rule, the legal basis, and the trap it targets. Progress is saved on this device only.
meta.disclaimer: Educational tool, not legal advice. Grounded in a research brief with a 23 July 2026 cut-off; always verify against the consolidated CRR on EUR-Lex and current EBA / Finansinspektionen material before relying on a point.

authority_ladder.example: Delegated regulations, RTS, ITS (e.g. IRB assessment methodology RTS)
EBA GL/2017/16; comply-or-explain, not Level 1 law
ECB Guide to internal models — persuasive, mainly for SSM banks
EBA Q&A, Interactive Single Rulebook — no binding force in law
GCD representativeness guide — a lens, never the legal basis

modules.name: Legal hierarchy & source status
modules.blurb: Tell binding law from guidelines, Q&As, ECB guides and industry material.

modules.name: Credit-risk architecture
modules.blurb: EL, ELBE, prudential vs accounting loss, shortfall/excess.

modules.name: IRB scope under CRR3
modules.blurb: Where IRB is narrowed, equity treatment, own-LGD/CCF, input floors.

modules.name: PD, LGD, downturn & MoC
modules.blurb: Discrimination vs calibration, downturn, margin of conservatism, representativeness.

modules.name: Output floor & transitionals
modules.blurb: A bank-wide floor, the phase-in ladder, and CRR3 transitional reliefs.

modules.name: Swedish overlay & supervisors
modules.blurb: Article 458 floors and qualifying ECB relevance for non-SSM banks.

modules.name: ML, representativeness & model change
modules.blurb: Principle-based ML expectations; model-change governance; industry data.

questions.prompt: A colleague cites an EBA guideline as "the binding rule" and ignores the CRR text on the point. What is the best correction?
options: The guideline overrides the CRR because it is more specific.
The CRR is binding legislation; an EBA guideline is not Level 1 law, though it still matters in supervision (comply-or-explain).
Both are equally binding sources of law.
Neither is binding; only ECB guides bind banks.
questions.explanation: Authority status comes first. EBA guidelines matter greatly in supervisory practice, but they are not Level 1 legislation. (EBA Q&As are weaker still — no binding force in law.)

questions.prompt: What is the legal status of an EBA Single Rulebook Q&A answer?
options: It is binding law once published.
It has no binding force in law, though it has practical significance and is scrutinised in application.
It binds only ECB-supervised banks.
It amends the CRR article it interprets.
questions.explanation: The EBA states directly that Q&A answers have no binding force in law. They are useful interpretive aids, never the sole legal basis for a "correct" answer.

questions.prompt: What is the status of the 2026 EBA amendment to the definition-of-default guidelines (as of the 23 July 2026 cut-off)?
options: Fully applicable and supersedes the 2016 package.
Published, but not yet applicable.
Only a consultation paper.
Withdrawn.
questions.explanation: The amended guideline package is final but not yet applicable. Teach version-awareness — do not collapse the 2016 and 2026 states into one "rule".

questions.prompt: Which of these is binding Level 1 legislation?
options: An EBA guideline (GL/2017/16)
The ECB Guide to internal models
The CRR (Regulation (EU) No 575/2013) as amended by CRR3
An entry in the EBA Interactive Single Rulebook
questions.explanation: Only the CRR (and its amending regulations such as CRR3) is Level 1 law here. The EBA guideline is Level 3, the ECB guide is supervisory interpretation, and the Interactive Single Rulebook is a documentation tool with no legal effect.

questions.prompt: A user says "the Interactive Single Rulebook is the authentic legal text." Correct?
options: Yes, it is the authentic version.
No — it is a navigation/documentation tool with no legal effect; the authentic texts are the Official Journal acts on EUR-Lex.
Yes, but only for RTS.
It is binding only in Sweden.
questions.explanation: The EBA states the Interactive Single Rulebook has no legal effect; the authentic versions are the OJ texts on EUR-Lex. Consolidated texts are documentation tools, not the authentic law.
questions.legal_basis: EBA Interactive Single Rulebook notice; EUR-Lex

questions.prompt: Which statement best distinguishes IFRS 9 ECL from prudential EL under the CRR?
options: They are the same number computed twice.
They interact, but are not the same concept; prudential EL is determined under CRR Articles 158-159.
Prudential EL is just IFRS 9 ECL plus a 10% add-on.
IFRS 9 ECL replaces prudential EL under CRR3.
questions.explanation: Accounting ECL and regulatory EL interact (via the shortfall/excess logic) but are distinct concepts. EBA's ECL guidelines explicitly do not determine expected losses for regulatory capital; CRR sets prudential EL.

questions.prompt: For a defaulted exposure, which parameter captures the best estimate of expected loss?
options: The non-defaulted EL from PD × LGD × EAD.
ELBE (the best estimate of expected loss), alongside LGD-in-default.
The output floor.
The IFRS 9 stage-1 ECL.
questions.explanation: For defaulted exposures the relevant concepts are ELBE (best estimate of expected loss) and LGD-in-default — distinct from the PD×LGD EL used for non-defaulted exposures.
questions.misconception: Using non-defaulted EL for defaulted exposures

questions.prompt: Under the IRB EL vs provisions comparison, what happens when regulatory EL exceeds eligible credit-risk adjustments (a shortfall)?
options: Nothing; EL and provisions are unrelated.
The shortfall is deducted from CET1 (Common Equity Tier 1).
The shortfall is added to CET1.
The exposure moves to the standardised approach.
questions.explanation: An IRB EL shortfall (EL greater than provisions) is deducted from CET1; an excess (provisions greater than EL) may be added to Tier 2 up to a cap. This is prudential logic, separate from accounting adjustments.
questions.misconception: Treating provisions and regulatory EL as the same thing

questions.prompt: A bank wants a new permission in 2026 to use IRB for equity exposures. What should the tutor say?
options: Fine — IRB equity permissions are unrestricted.
New permissions for IRB equity treatment should not be granted after 31 December 2024.
Only ECB can grant it.
Equity exposures are exempt from own funds requirements.
questions.explanation: CRR3 transitional provisions end the prospect of new permissions for IRB equity treatment after 31 December 2024; equity exposures move toward standardised treatment.
questions.misconception: Assuming pre-CRR3 IRB scope still applies

questions.prompt: Under CRR3, which is true about own-LGD / own-CCF use in the IRB approach?
options: Every exposure class may always use own estimates.
Own-LGD/own-CCF availability is more selective by exposure class; some exposures revert to Article 161 supervisory values or SA conversion factors.
Own estimates are abolished entirely.
Own estimates are only allowed for sovereigns.
questions.explanation: CRR3 narrows internal-estimation scope. Some exposure classes must use the supervisory LGD values in Article 161 or standardised CCFs rather than own estimates — teach which portfolios remain eligible.
questions.legal_basis: CRR Arts. 161, 166 (own estimates scope under CRR3)

questions.prompt: What did CRR3 introduce to constrain unduly low IRB parameter estimates?
options: A ban on internal models.
Explicit PD, LGD and CCF input floors.
A single flat 100% risk weight.
Nothing — parameters are unconstrained.
questions.explanation: CRR3 adds explicit PD, LGD and CCF input floors so internal estimates cannot fall below regulatory minimums (with some transitional relief, e.g. specialised lending under Article 495b).
questions.legal_basis: CRR Arts. 160-164 input floors; Art. 495b relief

questions.prompt: Under the current CRR3 text, what LGD input floor applies to retail exposures secured by residential property?
questions.explanation: CRR3 introduces explicit LGD floors for retail exposures and sets the floor for retail exposures secured by residential property at 5%.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are elements of the CRR definition of default? Select all that apply.
options: A material exposure more than 90 days past due.
Indications of unlikeliness to pay (UTP).
The exposure being profitable for the bank.
Consistent application across the obligor group where required.
questions.explanation: Default rests on the days-past-due criterion and unlikeliness-to-pay, with consistency of application (including at group/obligor level where required) and defined return-to-performing rules. Profitability is irrelevant.
questions.status_note: Binding law + Level 3 guideline (2026 amendment not yet applicable)

questions.prompt: A factoring case turns on a recent change to the definition-of-default guidance. What is the safe way to reason about it in mid-2026?
options: Apply the 2026 amended guideline as if fully in force.
Apply the currently applicable definition, and flag the 2026 amendment as adopted-but-not-yet-applicable.
Ignore the definition of default for factoring.
Treat industry practice as controlling.
questions.explanation: The 2026 amendment is not yet applicable, so the currently applicable definition governs. Good practice tracks current vs future-adopted states separately rather than teaching a not-yet-applicable rule as operative.
questions.legal_basis: CRR Art. 178; EBA definition-of-default guideline activity page

questions.prompt: A PD model has a high AUC but its predicted default rates run well below realised rates. What is the correct reading?
options: High AUC proves the model is compliant.
Discrimination (ranking power) and calibration (level accuracy) are separate properties; strong ranking does not fix a calibration problem.
Calibration is irrelevant if discrimination is strong.
The model must be discarded automatically.
questions.explanation: Ranking power and level calibration are distinct and must be evaluated separately. A model can rank well yet be mis-calibrated — a classic quant misconception that supervisory material warns against.
questions.legal_basis: EBA GL/2017/16; ECB Guide to internal models

questions.prompt: Which best describes the Margin of Conservatism (MoC)?
options: An ad hoc override applied to individual awkward cases.
A structural add-on addressing model and data uncertainty (e.g. deficiencies, estimation error), not case-by-case conservatism.
A way to lower parameter estimates.
The same thing as the output floor.
questions.explanation: MoC addresses identified deficiencies and general estimation uncertainty in a structured way. It is not ad hoc conservatism on individual exposures — a distinction that needs repeated remediation.

questions.prompt: What is the point of a downturn LGD estimate?
options: To reflect average conditions over the cycle.
To reflect economic downturn conditions, so it may exceed the long-run average LGD.
To minimise capital.
To replace the definition of default.
questions.explanation: Downturn LGD reflects adverse economic conditions and can exceed the long-run average. Identifying the downturn period and quantifying the adjustment is the subject of a dedicated EBA guideline.

questions.prompt: Which statements are supportable from current official sources on representativeness? Select all that apply.
options: One universal metric proves representativeness.
Context matters, including portfolio composition and use case.
Industry guidance (e.g. the GCD guide) may help as a practical lens.
The GCD guide can be the primary legal basis for a "correct" answer.
questions.explanation: Official sources are objective- and context-based; there is no single mandatory metric that "proves" representativeness, and industry guidance is a lens, never the legal basis. So B and C only.
questions.legal_basis: EBA GL/2017/16; research brief (GCD as labelled industry lens)

questions.prompt: An analyst says the output floor means every IRB mortgage exposure must carry at least a fixed minimum risk weight. Most accurate reply?
options: Correct — it is an exposure-level minimum risk weight.
The output floor is a bank-wide floor on total own-funds requirements derived from standardised-equivalent calculations, not an exposure-level minimum.
It only applies to sovereigns.
It was abolished by CRR3.
questions.explanation: CRR3 frames the output floor as a limit on overall own-funds requirements from internal models relative to the standardised approach, computed bank-wide. Teaching it as an exposure-level minimum misstates the mechanism.

questions.prompt: On the output-floor phase-in ladder, which factor applies in 2026?
questions.explanation: The Article 465 phase-in runs 50% (2025), 55% (2026), 60% (2027), 65% (2028), 70% (2029), reaching 72.5% from 2030. Always state whether a question is current-year, transitional or fully loaded.

questions.prompt: What is the fully phased-in output-floor factor from 2030 onwards?
questions.explanation: From 2030 the output floor is 72.5% of the standardised-equivalent own-funds requirement, once the annual transitionals have expired.

questions.prompt: In 2026 an IRB bank asks whether an eligible unrated corporate exposure can get a favourable output-floor transitional risk weight if its PD estimate is low enough. Is this current law or just policy discussion?
options: Only a consultation idea.
Current transitional law — a 65% risk weight for eligible unrated corporates under the output floor, until 31 December 2032 where the PD condition is met.
Never permitted.
Only for sovereigns.
questions.explanation: Article 465(3) allows a 65% risk weight for certain unrated corporates in the output-floor calculation until 31 December 2032 when the PD condition is met. It is transitional law, not mere policy discussion.

questions.prompt: Why must even advanced IRB quants now have strong Standardised Approach literacy?
options: Because IRB has been abolished.
Because the output floor forces internal-model banks to compute standardised-equivalent requirements bank-wide.
Because the SA is optional trivia.
Because ECB guides require it for Swedish LSIs.
questions.explanation: The post-CRR3 learning problem is broader than IRB doctrine: the output floor makes SA classification, real-estate treatment and conversion factors directly relevant to internal-model banks.

questions.prompt: As of 23 July 2026, which statement about Swedish mortgage risk-weight floors is correct?
options: There is no Swedish-specific floor; only EU rules apply.
For IRB banks, the Swedish Article 458 mortgage floor remains 25% and has been extended to 30 December 2027.
The floor is 15% and expired in 2024.
It applies only to standardised-approach banks.
questions.explanation: Finansinspektionen extended the Swedish Article 458 mortgage floor (25%) to 30 December 2027, and it applies to banks using the IRB approach. These are jurisdiction-specific national measures, not generic EU rules.
questions.legal_basis: CRR Art. 458; FI decision of 17 Sep 2025

questions.prompt: Under the Swedish Article 458 measures for IRB banks, what is the floor for Swedish corporate exposures secured on commercial property?
options: No floor
questions.explanation: The Swedish floors are 25% for mortgages, 35% for corporate exposures secured on commercial property, and 25% for corporate exposures secured on residential property. A Swedish tutor must branch by jurisdiction and portfolio.

questions.prompt: A Swedish bank outside direct ECB supervision copies an ECB Guide paragraph into a policy and labels it "binding requirement in Sweden." Best review comment?
options: Leave it — the ECB guide is binding law everywhere.
Re-label it as supervisory guidance / persuasive interpretation, unless the same proposition is grounded in binding law, EBA material, or FI practice.
Delete all reference to supervisory expectations.
Replace it with a GCD citation.
questions.explanation: The ECB guide explains how the ECB reads applicable rules for directly supervised institutions; it does not itself create binding law for all Swedish institutions. For a non-SSM bank it is persuasive, not automatically operative.
questions.legal_basis: ECB Guide to internal models; CRR
questions.misconception: ECB guide binds Swedish banks automatically

questions.prompt: What is the official stance on using machine learning in IRB models?
options: ML is prohibited for IRB.
The stance is conditional and principle-based — centred on explainability, governance, validation and compliance with prudential requirements.
ML is mandatory under CRR3.
ML is unregulated.
questions.explanation: EBA's discussion paper and 2023 follow-up, and the 2025 ECB guide, frame ML through explainability, governance and validation rather than a ban. ML is a prudential-compliance problem, not a yes/no technology question.
questions.legal_basis: EBA ML discussion paper & 2023 follow-up; ECB Guide to internal models

questions.prompt: A bank proposes an ML model with automatic frequent retraining and argues model-change governance is irrelevant because the algorithm family is unchanged. Best response?
options: Agreed — unchanged algorithm family means no model change.
Frequent or automatic updates can still create material model-change issues and require careful classification and governance.
Model changes never matter for ML.
Only the ECB can decide.
questions.explanation: Update frequency, stability, and resulting changes in ordering/distribution are key prudential concerns. A stable algorithm family does not exempt a model from material-model-change classification and governance.
questions.legal_basis: RTS on material model changes; EBA ML follow-up report
questions.status_note: Binding Level 2 (current RTS; 2026 amendments still in draft)
questions.misconception: Assuming stable architecture means no model change

questions.prompt: How should the GCD representativeness guide be used when deciding a compliance answer?
options: As the primary legal basis for the correct answer.
As a labelled industry lens for practical reasoning and scenario design — never as the legal basis.
As binding EBA material.
It must be ignored entirely.
questions.explanation: Industry guidance can support practical reasoning and realism, but cannot define the legally correct answer. The legal basis stays with the CRR and adopted secondary law.
questions.legal_basis: Research brief (GCD as labelled industry guidance); EBA GL/2017/16
questions.status_note: Industry material — not a legal basis
questions.misconception: GCD tells us what the law requires
